result: I keep thinking about the way light pools on my kitchen floor at 4:17 p.m., how it arrives unannounced like a friend who lets themselves in with the spare key. There’s something conspiratorial in that glow, as if the sun and the cracked linoleum have been scheming all day to remind me that wonder doesn’t need an invitation. I stand there barefoot, coffee gone cold, while the dust motes perform slow-motion cartwheels and the neighbour’s radio leaks mariachi through the window screen. Time feels porous in these intervals; minutes leak into each other the way wet watercolours blur on cheap paper. I find myself narrating my own gestures—she closes the refrigerator door too hard, she misplaces her pen behind the cereal again—like I’m both the protagonist and the unreliable narrator of a novella that refuses to resolve. Somewhere between the sink and the back door I remember my mother measuring my height against the doorframe, the pencil mark smudged by decades of opening and closing. I’m still growing, just sideways now, expanding in memories rather than inches. The light shifts by a degree, signalling the planet’s indifferent rotation, and I decide to stay right here until the gold fades entirely, until the kitchen forgets I was ever present, letting the room settle back into its anonymous blue-grey evening skin, the day sealing itself like an envelope already addressed to tomorrow.
